content: $$\begin{aligned} &{ }_5 \mathrm{~B}^{10}+{ }_2 \mathrm{He}^4 \rightarrow \mathrm{X}+{ }_0 \mathrm{n}^1\\\\ &\text { In the above nuclear reaction ' } X \text { ' will be } \end{aligned}$$
explanation:
We are given the nuclear reaction:

$ {}_5\mathrm{B}^{10} + {}_2\mathrm{He}^4 \rightarrow X + {}_0\mathrm{n}^1 $

We need to determine what $ X $ is.

Step 1. Write down the total atomic number (protons)

On the left side:

$ 5 (from~B) + 2 (from~He) = 7 $

On the right side:

If the neutron is ${}_0\mathrm{n}^1$, it contributes 0 protons.

So the unknown nucleus $ X $ must have:

$ Z_X = 7 $

That means $ X $ is nitrogen ($\mathrm{N}$).

Step 2. Write down the total mass number (nucleons)

On the left side:

$ 10 (from~B) + 4 (from~He) = 14 $

On the right side:

$ A_X + 1 (from~neutron) $

Equating both sides:

$ A_X + 1 = 14 \Rightarrow A_X = 13 $

Step 3. Identify $ X $

$ X = {}_7\mathrm{N}^{13} $

✅ Answer: Option B

$ \boxed{{ }_7\mathrm{N}^{13}} $

content: After the emission of a $$\beta$$-particle followed by an $$\alpha$$-particle from $${ }_{83}^{214} \mathrm{Bi}$$, the number of neutrons in the atom is -
explanation:
To determine the number of neutrons in the atom after the emission of a $$\beta$$-particle and an $$\alpha$$-particle from $${ }_{83}^{214} \mathrm{Bi}$$, we need to understand how these emissions affect the atomic and mass numbers.

1. Emission of a $$\beta$$-particle:

A $$\beta$$-particle is essentially an electron, and its emission converts a neutron into a proton. Therefore, the atomic number increases by 1, but the mass number remains the same. For $${ }_{83}^{214} \mathrm{Bi}$$ (bismuth), after the emission of a $$\beta$$-particle, the atomic number becomes 84:

$$ { }_{83}^{214} \mathrm{Bi} \rightarrow { }_{84}^{214} \mathrm{Po} + \beta $$

2. Emission of an $$\alpha$$-particle:

An $$\alpha$$-particle consists of 2 protons and 2 neutrons, so its emission decreases the atomic number by 2 and the mass number by 4. Therefore, for $${ }_{84}^{214} \mathrm{Po}$$ (polonium), after the emission of an $$\alpha$$-particle, the atomic number becomes 82 and the mass number becomes 210:

$$ { }_{84}^{214} \mathrm{Po} \rightarrow { }_{82}^{210} \mathrm{Pb} + \alpha $$

Now, we need to find the number of neutrons in the resulting atom, $${ }_{82}^{210} \mathrm{Pb}$$ (lead). The number of neutrons is given by the difference between the mass number and the atomic number:

$$ \text{Number of neutrons} = \text{Mass number} - \text{Atomic number} $$

$$ \text{Number of neutrons} = 210 - 82 = 128 $$

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option B

128

content: If in case of a radio isotope the value of half-life (T$$_{1/2}$$) and decay constant ($$\lambda$$) are identical in magnitude, then their value should be
explanation:
For radioactive decay, half-life is given by

$$T_{1 / 2}=\frac{0.693}{\lambda}$$ .... (i)

Also, $$T_{1 / 2}=\lambda$$ (Given)

Let both of them are equal to $$x$$, then Eq. (1) will become

$$\begin{aligned} & x =\frac{0.693}{x} \Rightarrow x^2=0.693 \\ \text { or } & \quad x =(0.693)^{1 / 2} \\ \Rightarrow & \quad T_{1 / 2} =\lambda=(0.693)^{1 / 2} \end{aligned}$$

content: For the reaction $$_7^{14}N(\alpha ,p)\,{}^{17}O$$, 1.16 MeV (Mass equivalent = 0.00124 amu) of energy is absorbed. Mass on the reactant side is 18.00567 amu and proton mass = 1.00782 amu. The atomic mass of $${}^{17}O$$ will be
explanation:
Mass on the reactant side = 18.00567 amu

Mass of the proton = 1.00782 amu

Energy absorbed in the reaction = 1.16 MeV = 0.00124 amu

$${}^{14}N(\alpha ,p)\,{}^{17}O$$

This reaction can be simplified as

$$_7^{14}N + _2^4\alpha \buildrel {\Delta E} \over \longrightarrow _8^{17}O + _1^1H$$

where, $$\Delta$$E is the energy absorbed.

In this reaction, highly energetic alpha particle with kinetic energy 1.16 MeV are absorbed by $$_7^{14}N$$ nucleus to form $$_8^{17}O$$ isotope and a proton.

Hence,


According to conservation of mass-energy,

18.00567 amu = ($$_8^{17}O$$ + 1.0078 $$-$$ 0.00124) amu

$$_8^{17}O$$ = 18.00567 $$-$$ 1.0078 + 0.00124 = 16.99914 amu

content: The half-life period of $${}_{53}{I^{125}}$$ is 60 days. The radioactivity after 180 days will be
explanation:
Let, initial concentration (a) = 100

Given, half-life (t1/2) = 60 days

To find, radioactivity,

i.e., (a $$-$$ x) after time T (180 days)

$$\because$$ T = n $$\times$$ t1/2

where, n = no. of half-lives

180 = n $$\times$$ 60

$$n = {{180} \over {60}} = 3$$

and $$(a - x) = {a \over {{2^n}}} = {{100} \over {{2^3}}} = {{100} \over 8}$$

$$(a - x) = 12.5\% $$

Hence, (b) is the correct answer.

content:
Consider, the radioactive disintegration

$${}_{82}{A^{210}}\buildrel {} \over \longrightarrow B\buildrel {} \over \longrightarrow C\buildrel {} \over \longrightarrow {}_{82}{A^{206}}$$

The sequence of emission can be
explanation:
Given, $${}_{82}{A^{210}}\buildrel {} \over \longrightarrow B\buildrel {} \over \longrightarrow C\buildrel {} \over \longrightarrow {}_{82}{A^{206}}$$ i.e. difference in mass no. between A and D is of 4 units i.e., mass no. is decreased by 4 units while atomic no. remains the same, i.e. 82 for A and D. Also,

(i) On emission of each $$\beta$$-particle, mass no. remains the same but atomic no. is increased by one unit.

(b) On emission of each $$\alpha$$-particle, mass no. is decreased by 4 units, while atomic no. is decreased by 2 units.

Thus, on emission of 2-$$\beta$$ and 1-$$\alpha$$ particle, we get $${}_{82}{A^{206}}$$,

i.e. $${}_{82}{A^{210}}\buildrel { - \beta } \over \longrightarrow {}_{83}{A^{210}}\buildrel { - \beta } \over \longrightarrow {}_{84}{A^{210}}C\buildrel { - \alpha } \over \longrightarrow {}_{82}{A^{206}}$$

Thus, option (d) is the correct answer.

content: Which atomic species cannot be used as a nuclear fuel?
explanation: Among the given options, $${}_{92}{U^{238}}$$ is an isotope of uranium but cannot be used as a nuclear-fuel. U-238 is non-fissile, i.e., it does not undergo fission by thermal neutrons. The energy released when U-238 absorbs a neutron is insufficient to carry out nuclear fission. Hence, (d) is the correct option.

content: The half-life of C<sup>14</sup> is 5760 years. For a 200 mg sample of C<sup>14</sup>, the time taken to change to 25 mg is
explanation:
Given,

Hlf-life of C14, t1/2 = 5760 years

Initial concentration of sample of C14, N0 = 200 mg

Final concentration of sample of C14, Nt = 25 mg

The given decay is radioactive and all radioactive decay follows first order kinetics.

$${t_{1/2}} = {{0.693} \over \lambda }$$

$$\therefore$$ $$\lambda = {{0.693} \over {{t_{1/2}}}} = {{0.693} \over {5760}}y{r^{ - 1}}$$

We know that, for first order reaction

$$t = {{2.303} \over \lambda }\log {{{N_0}} \over {{N_t}}} = {{2.303} \over {{{0.693} \over {5760}}}}\log {{[200]} \over {[25]}}$$

$$ = {{2.303 \times 5760} \over {0.693}}\log 8$$

= 17,286.78 yrs $$ \approx $$ 17,280 yrs

Therefore, the time taken to change 200 mg to 25 mg is 17,286.78 yr, which is very close to option (d) i.e. 17280 yrs.

content: The nucleus $$_{29}^{64}$$Cu accepts an orbital electron to yield,
explanation:
The nucleus $$_{29}^{64}$$Cu accepts an orbital electron to yield $$_{28}^{64}$$Ni. The atomic number of Cu is 29, which is equal to the number of electrons and also equal to the number of protons. When $$_{29}^{64}$$Cu accepts an orbital electron then electrons subtract from the atomic number, i.e. 29$$-$$1 = 28

$$_{29}^{64}$$Cu + $$-$$1e0$$\to$$ $$_{28}^{64}$$Ni

content: C<sub>6</sub>H<sub>5</sub>F<sup>18</sup> is a F<sup>18</sup> radio-isotope labelled organic compound. F<sup>18</sup> decays by positron emission. The product resulting on decay is
explanation:
$${}_9{F^{18}} \to {}_x{E^y} + {}_{ + 1}{e^0}$$

x = 8, y = 18

$$\therefore$$ $${}_x{E^y}$$ = $${}_8{O^{18}}$$

$$\because$$ Position has one unit of the charge and zero mass.

Thus, F18 changes to O18 therefore resulting decy product is $${C_6}{H_5}{O^{18}}$$.

content: If radium and chlorine combine to form radium chloride, the compound would be
explanation:
Radium in isolated form and in its chloride (i.e. Ra+) only differ in the number of orbital electron.

But, radioactivity is independent of chemical environment of an ion or atom, i.e. radioactivity is the phenomenon which does not depend on the orbital electrons but depends only on the composition of nucleus.

content: An element E loses one $$\alpha$$ and two $$\beta$$-particles in three successive stages. The resulting element will be
explanation:
Let Z = atomic number

M = atomic mass

$$_Z^ME\buildrel { - \alpha } \over \longrightarrow {}_{Z - 2}{E^{M - 4}}\buildrel { - 2\beta } \over \longrightarrow {}_Z{E^{M - 4}}$$

Hence, $$_Z^ME$$ and $${}_2{E^{M - 4}}$$ has same atomic number with different atomic mass thus, they are isotopes.
